network.overall_strategy: The network centers cysteine synthesis on the literature-supported biomimetic route of Foden et al. (2020): cyanosulfidic production of glycolaldehyde feeds Strecker synthesis of serine nitrile, followed by chemoselective N,O-diacetylation, near-neutral-pH elimination to N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile, and sulfide addition to cysteine derivatives that hydrolyze/deprotect to cysteine. Hydrothermal chemistry contributes reductants, sulfide, mineral interfaces, and a minor trace direct cysteine branch from the Hennet-Holm-Engel pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite system. Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry supplies the dominant carbon-nitrogen backbone, while biochemical/prebiotic steps convert cysteine derivatives into free cysteine and downstream sulfur-rich products. Ten distinct pathways are represented as different valid routes through the same connected bipartite graph, with strong convergence at HCN, glycolaldehyde, serine nitrile, N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile, and H2S. Direct Strecker synthesis of cysteine is explicitly excluded as a productive route because the literature shows cysteine nitrile is intrinsically unstable; beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde instead diverts to 2-aminothiazole selection chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal serpentinization-linked sulfide and hydrogen supply

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, serpentinization-driven reducing environment

reactions.mechanism: Serpentinization and sulfide-rich vent circulation generate H2-rich, H2S-bearing fluids that feed downstream carbon and sulfur chemistry rather than directly fixing carbon to cysteine.
reactions.reasoning: Alkaline hydrothermal vents generated by serpentinization provide H2-rich, sulfide-bearing, moderately warm settings more compatible with amino-acid survival than black smokers; FeS/Fe3S4 phases are central mineral components in these systems (Russell & Hall, 1997; Martin et al., 2008; White et al., 2015). This reaction is included as an environmental source term to connect hydrothermal sulfur/reductant supply to surface cyanosulfidic and biomimetic cysteine chemistry.

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic homologation of HCN to formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfide and mineral photocatalytic surfaces
conditions.other: UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic conditions with ferrocyanide/Cu photoredox analogs in the literature

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox-driven reductive homologation of HCN under sulfide-rich conditions generates simple aldehydes, with glycolaldehyde as a key branch-point product.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanosulfidic protometabolism generates glycolaldehyde from HCN using UV light and sulfide-based photoredox cycling, providing the principal upstream feedstock for serine nitrile and ribonucleotide precursor chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018; Green et al., 2021). Mineral agents are specified here to satisfy the required surface-mineral encoding while remaining consistent with UV-driven surface settings.

reactions.name: Further cyanosulfidic homologation of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with borate stabilization of sugars
conditions.other: UV irradiation in cyanosulfidic pools; borate helps stabilize higher sugars

reactions.mechanism: Successive reductive homologation extends the carbon chain from glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde in a shared photochemical network.
reactions.reasoning: The Sutherland network identifies glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde as central homologation products formed in the same cyanosulfidic system, linking amino acid and nucleotide precursor chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Patel et al., 2015; Green et al., 2021). Borate stabilization is consistent with prebiotic sugar handling in surface settings.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of serine nitrile from glycolaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde imine formation with ammonia is followed by cyanide addition to produce the alpha-aminonitrile serine nitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. identify serine nitrile as the correct upstream entry point to prebiotic cysteine chemistry, and glycolaldehyde is a well-established Strecker precursor to serine nitrile in the Sutherland/Powner framework (Foden et al., 2020; Patel et al., 2015). This is the productive route, in contrast to the failed direct Strecker route to cysteine.

reactions.name: Chemoselective N,O-diacetylation of serine nitrile

conditions.other: pH 7; 1 hour; chemoselective acyl transfer by thioacetic acid

reactions.mechanism: Thioacetic acid transfers acetyl groups to both the amino and hydroxyl functions of serine nitrile under mild aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key activating step of the Powner biomimetic pathway: serine nitrile is converted to N,O-diacetylserine nitrile in 91% yield at pH 7 and room temperature using thioacetic acid and ferricyanide (Foden et al., 2020). Thioacetic acid acts as both acyl donor and sulfur source.

reactions.name: Beta-elimination to N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile
reactions.mechanism: The alpha-nitrile activates near-neutral-pH beta-elimination from O-acetyl serine nitrile to the unusually stable dehydroalanine nitrile derivative.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. showed that N,O-diacetylserine nitrile eliminates at pH 8 and room temperature to N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile in 85% conversion, whereas the corresponding amide does not. This nitrile-enabled elimination is the central breakthrough solving the cysteine problem (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Alternative phosphoserine-like elimination route to dehydroalanine nitrile
reactions.mechanism: An alternative elimination manifold under Mg2+-assisted warm aqueous conditions also accesses the same dehydroalanine nitrile hub, though less efficiently.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. reported an alternative phosphoserine-inspired route giving N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile under Mg2+ (40 mM) at 60C in 24% yield. It is a lower-yield but literature-supported convergent access to the same hub intermediate (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Sulfide addition to N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile
reactions.mechanism: Hydrosulfide performs conjugate addition to the activated dehydroalanine nitrile, generating the cysteine thioamide derivative.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. showed that N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile reacts with H2S in near-quantitative yield to give N-acetylcysteine thioamide under mild aqueous conditions. This is the decisive sulfur incorporation step (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Thioacetic-acid addition to dehydroalanine nitrile leading to acetylated cysteine derivative

conditions.catalyst: aqueous thioester-forming addition followed by hydrolytic workup
conditions.other: pH 7; ~12 hours for quantitative thioester formation; connected here to the acetylated cysteine pool

reactions.mechanism: Thioacetic acid adds across the activated alkene to form an acetylated cysteine precursor pool that can furnish N-acetylcysteine-related products.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. reported that N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile reacts quantitatively with thioacetic acid at pH 7 to yield a cysteine thioester manifold; subsequent ammonolysis or thiolysis furnishes cysteine derivatives. This reaction is represented as converging into the N-acetylcysteine derivative pool for network connectivity while preserving the experimentally established sulfur-addition chemistry (Foden et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis/deprotection of N-acetylcysteine thioamide to N-acetylcysteine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Thioamide hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions converts the sulfur-incorporated nitrile-derived intermediate into the stable acetylated amino acid pool.
reactions.reasoning: Foden et al. established sulfur addition products such as N-acetylcysteine thioamide and related acetylated cysteine derivatives as accessible endpoints of the biomimetic sequence. Their conversion to the N-acetylcysteine pool is a necessary connecting hydrolysis/deprotection step and is therefore included as a conservative downstream transformation consistent with the reported product manifold (Foden et al., 2020; explicitly connective but less directly quantified than the preceding steps).

reactions.name: Deacetylation of N-acetylcysteine to cysteine

conditions.other: mild hydrolytic deacylation in concentrated prebiotic pools or compartments

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic cleavage of the N-acetyl group liberates free cysteine from the protected cysteine reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: Free cysteine is expected to arise from deprotection/hydrolysis of the acetylated cysteine products generated in the Foden sequence. This step is not the core breakthrough itself but is a necessary connection from the experimentally demonstrated acetylated cysteine chemistry to the target molecule; it is included as a cautious downstream hydrolytic step motivated by the cysteine-derivative manifold in Foden et al. (2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal trace cysteine formation in pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite system

conditions.other: 150C; NH4Cl-containing hydrothermal experiment; trace-to-present cysteine among amino acid products

reactions.mechanism: Complex aqueous Strecker-like and condensation chemistry in the presence of sulfide minerals yields a mixture of amino acids with only trace cysteine formation.
reactions.reasoning: Hennet, Holm, and Engel reported one of the very few hydrothermal mineral-catalyzed systems to detect cysteine, using HCN, formaldehyde, and NH4Cl with a pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite assemblage at 150C and 10 bar. The cysteine yield was only trace-to-present and mechanistically unresolved, so this branch is explicitly minor and not the main route to the target (Hennet et al., 1992).

reactions.name: Sulfur diversion of glycolaldehyde to beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde

conditions.other: sulfur-dependent branch competing with serine-precursor chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Sulfur-rich surface chemistry diverts glycolaldehyde into the sulfur aldehyde beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde rather than onward productive cysteine synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The literature highlights that when sulfide is present, glycolaldehyde can be diverted toward beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde, creating a sulfur-dependent branch away from serine nitrile and toward heterocyclic selection chemistry rather than productive cysteine Strecker synthesis (Foden et al., 2020; Powner et al., 2012). This is included as a documented diversion, not a main cysteine route.

reactions.name: Formation of 2-aminothiazole from beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde and cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: aqueous concentration and crystallization on mineral-rich shores
conditions.other: 2-aminothiazole crystallizes directly from concentrated aqueous solution

reactions.mechanism: Cyclocondensation of beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde with cyanamide forms the thiazole heterocycle used in prebiotic selection chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Powner and co-workers showed that beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde reacts cleanly with cyanamide to form 2-aminothiazole, which crystallizes from water and plays a major role in sugar selection chemistry. This branch is explicitly important because it explains why sulfur aldehyde chemistry does not solve cysteine synthesis directly (Powner et al., 2012; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: UV photochemical direct formation of cysteine and cystine from reduced atmospheric mixtures

conditions.other: represents UV photolysis of reduced CH4/C2H6/NH3/H2O/H2S mixtures rather than a simple two-component system; low yield

reactions.mechanism: H2S-sensitized UV radical chemistry in reduced gas-water systems generates low amounts of sulfur amino acids including cysteine/cystine.
reactions.reasoning: Khare & Sagan (1971) and Sagan & Khare (1971) directly detected cysteine/cystine from UV photolysis experiments using reduced mixtures containing hydrocarbons, NH3, H2O, and H2S, with H2S acting as a photosensitizer. This branch is retained as a low-yield classical route and is intentionally encoded as a broad atmospheric-mixture pathway rather than an invalid underspecified one-step conversion (Khare & Sagan, 1971; Sagan & Khare, 1971).

reactions.name: Decarboxylation of cysteine to cysteamine

conditions.other: thermal or geochemically assisted decarboxylation in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Loss of the carboxyl group converts cysteine into cysteamine, linking amino acid sulfur chemistry to coenzyme-like thiol chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Cysteamine is the thiol moiety of coenzyme A and has long been considered a plausible prebiotic sulfur metabolite; the literature review explicitly highlights the cysteine-to-cysteamine connection in prebiotic networks (Miller & Schlesinger, 1993; Fairchild et al., 2024). This step is included as a downstream biochemical connection rather than as part of the core target-forming route.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic-to-biomimetic cysteine route
pathways.description: Dominant literature-supported route: HCN/H2S photoredox chemistry gives glycolaldehyde, which undergoes Strecker synthesis to serine nitrile and then the Foden biomimetic sequence to cysteine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal sulfur supply feeding canonical surface route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal vents provide sulfide and reducing conditions that feed the same productive surface biomimetic pathway.

pathways.name: Alternative Mg2+-assisted dehydroalanine access
pathways.description: Same upstream serine nitrile route, but elimination proceeds through the lower-yield Mg2+-assisted alternative reported by Foden et al.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed Mg2+ alternative route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal sulfide supply plus the Mg2+-assisted surface elimination branch to the same dehydroalanine nitrile hub.

pathways.name: Acetylated cysteine pool route via thioacetic-acid sulfur addition
pathways.description: After dehydroalanine nitrile formation, thioacetic acid provides an alternative sulfur-addition path into the acetylated cysteine pool before release of free cysteine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed acetylated cysteine pool route
pathways.description: Vent-derived sulfide supports the same productive route, but thioacetic acid is used at the sulfur-addition stage to reach N-acetylcysteine-related intermediates.

pathways.name: Alternative homologation-expanded route through glyceraldehyde branch
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic chemistry first expands to glyceraldehyde before the network reconnects through the shared glycolaldehyde/serine nitrile hub chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal trace direct cysteine route
pathways.description: Minor branch reflecting the Hennet-Holm-Engel hydrothermal experiment; included explicitly as trace-level and mechanistically unresolved.

pathways.name: Classical UV photochemical direct sulfur amino acid route
pathways.description: Low-yield direct formation of cysteine/cystine in reduced atmospheric photolysis experiments with H2S photosensitization.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface canonical route with downstream biochemical sulfur network
pathways.description: The main productive route continued into downstream sulfur biochemistry, illustrating how newly formed cysteine connects onward to cysteamine chemistry after target formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest pathway and closely matches the literature-supported Foden et al. biomimetic route. The sequence HCN/H2S photochemistry to glycolaldehyde, Strecker formation of serine nitrile, acetylation, elimination to N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile, and sulfide addition is highly coherent. The only mild weakness is that the final hydrolysis/deacetylation steps to free cysteine are less explicitly grounded in the cited primary breakthrough than the preceding steps.
suggestions: Specify whether rxn_010 proceeds via thioamide hydrolysis to N-acetylcysteine under neutral aqueous conditions or ammonolysis/thiolysis-derived branch chemistry from the Foden framework.
Clarify conditions and support for deacetylation in rxn_011, since release of free cysteine may require a distinct hydrolytic setting and poses stability concerns.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hydrothermal-to-surface handoff is conceptually strong, but rxn_001 is chemically weak as written because CO2 is not a realistic direct precursor to both H2 and H2S. Serpentinization can justify H2 production, but sulfide supply typically requires sulfur-bearing minerals or dissolved sulfur species, not CO2 alone. Downstream canonical cysteine chemistry remains strong.
suggestions: Rewrite rxn_001 to use water plus Fe-bearing ultramafic rock and sulfur-bearing phases as inputs, or treat H2 and H2S as environmental feedstocks rather than products of a single stoichiometric reaction.
Keep hydrothermal contribution as justified source of reductant/mineral interface rather than forcing an implausible balanced conversion from CO2.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This alternative route is well aligned with the literature so long as rxn_007 reflects the Mg2+-assisted phosphoserine-like elimination at elevated temperature. It is slightly weaker than P1 because the alternative elimination is lower-yield and more conditional than the canonical near-neutral nitrile-enabled elimination, but still plausible.
suggestions: Explicitly encode Mg2+ and 60C-like conditions in rxn_007 to match the literature-supported alternative elimination route.
Differentiate rxn_007 mechanistically from rxn_006 more clearly to avoid apparent duplication.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway inherits both the strengths of the biomimetic cysteine route and the main weakness of the hydrothermal sulfur-supply step. The environmental logic is acceptable, but the hydrothermal entry reaction is oversimplified and undermines confidence.
suggestions: Use geochemically realistic hydrothermal feedstock specification for sulfide and hydrogen generation.
State whether sulfide is transported from vent-derived fluids to surface pools or regenerated locally in sulfur-rich subaerial settings.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The thioacetic-acid branch is literature-consistent in spirit, but the mapping from Ac-Dha-CN + thioacetic acid directly to N-acetylcysteine is compressed too aggressively. Foden supports quantitative thioester formation and subsequent ammonolysis/thiolysis steps, not necessarily one-step direct access to the exact product listed here. Thus the branch is plausible but somewhat under-specified.
suggestions: Split rxn_009 into thioester formation and subsequent conversion steps to mirror the literature more faithfully.
Clarify whether the product is a cysteine thioester, Ac-Cys-CN, or Ac-Cys-SNH2 rather than collapsing several transformations.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is acceptable but weakened by both the problematic hydrothermal supply reaction and the compressed thioacetic-acid branch chemistry. It remains connected and target-directed, but is less rigorous than the canonical route.
suggestions: Repair the hydrothermal source chemistry.
Expand the thioacetic-acid branch into literature-grounded intermediate steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The network includes glyceraldehyde as an expanded branch from cyanosulfidic homologation, which is literature-supported, but its specific necessity for cysteine synthesis is weak. Because glyceraldehyde does not clearly improve access to serine nitrile here, the route feels more like network elaboration than a chemically essential cysteine pathway.
suggestions: Explain the functional role of glyceraldehyde in this pathway beyond demonstrating cyanosulfidic expansion.
If retained, connect glyceraldehyde more explicitly to feedstock diversification or selection chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The trace hydrothermal route is grounded in a real experimental report and is valuable as a minor branch, but it should remain explicitly low-confidence. Literature support exists only for trace-to-present cysteine in a pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite system, and the exact mechanism is uncertain. As a standalone route to target accumulation, it is weak.
suggestions: Keep this route labeled as trace/minor and avoid presenting it as a robust production pathway.
Specify the reported conditions more precisely: ~150C, 10 bar, HCN + formaldehyde + NH4Cl in the PPM mineral system.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct UV photochemical production of cysteine/cystine has classical precedent, but the reaction as encoded is far too compressed and chemically underspecified. The literature involves reduced atmospheric mixtures with CH4, hydrocarbons, NH3, H2O, and H2S under UV irradiation, not simply H2S + NH3. This branch is acceptable only as a very coarse historical side-route and not as a well-defined network edge.
suggestions: Add the missing atmospheric carbon sources and UV conditions explicitly if this branch is retained.
Consider treating this as empirical observational support rather than a mechanistically resolved synthetic step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a strong integrated route from hydrothermal feedstocks through surface synthesis into downstream biochemical sulfur chemistry. The extension to cysteamine is literature-relevant. However, the same hydrothermal source issue remains, and the biochemical release of free cysteine is less directly supported than the core Foden route itself.
suggestions: Refine hydrothermal feedstock chemistry.
Distinguish clearly between well-supported cysteine derivative formation and the more speculative liberation/stability of free cysteine before downstream decarboxylation.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong, well connected, and clearly centered on the literature-supported biomimetic cysteine synthesis of Foden et al. Its strongest feature is correct exclusion of direct productive Strecker access via beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde/cysteine nitrile, while retaining beta-mercaptoacetaldehyde as a diversion to 2-aminothiazole selection chemistry in line with Powner-group findings. The main canonical route is chemically persuasive and captures the key hubs HCN, glycolaldehyde, serine nitrile, N-acetyl-dehydroalanine nitrile, and sulfide. Environmental interconnection is also good overall: hydrothermal settings provide reductants/mineral context, surface cyanosulfidic chemistry builds the carbon-nitrogen skeleton, and biochemical steps release/use cysteine derivatives. However, several issues prevent approval at the >=0.9 threshold. The biggest problem is reaction encoding quality for certain auxiliary branches: rxn_001 is geochemically implausible as written because CO2 cannot directly generate both H2 and H2S; rxn_015 is overly compressed and omits essential atmospheric carbon inputs documented in the classical UV work; rxn_009 conflates several literature-supported transformations into one step; and rxn_011 lacks explicit support/conditions for clean deacetylation to free cysteine. A second issue is likely schema noncompliance relative to the original generation requirements: every Hydrothermal and Surface reaction should have specified mineral agents, but the evaluated summary omits them entirely, so full environmental-agent compliance cannot be verified. Literature alignment is otherwise strong for the central route and for the stated historical constraints. Overall, this is a good network with an excellent core pathway but notable reaction-definition weaknesses and some overstated side branches.
